'''Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (''kamitsu-unakami no kuni no miyatsuko'', ''kamitsu-unakami kokuzō'') was a {{ill|kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} who ruled {{ill|Unakami District (Kazusa Province)|ja|海上郡 (上総国)|lt=Unakami District|lt_ja=海上郡}} of {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}} (part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|ja|市原市|lt=Ichihara City|lt_ja=市原市}} in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}). Also written as Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko (上菟上国造).

== Overview ==
Along the {{ill|Yōrō River|ja|養老川}} basin of its territory lie the {{ill|Godo Kofun Cluster|ja|神門古墳群|lt=Godo Kofun Cluster|lt_ja=神門古墳群}} — {{ill|emerging-period kofun|ja|出現期古墳}} ({{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|纒向型前方後円墳}}) of the {{ill|3rd century|ja|3世紀}}<ref>Because treating these as kofun would substantially push back the beginning of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, there is also a theory treating them not as kofun but as {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}}.</ref> — and the {{ill|Inaridai No. 1 Kofun|ja|稲荷台1号古墳}}, which yielded the {{ill|king-gift iron sword|ja|鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|lt=king-gift inscribed iron sword|lt_ja=王賜銘}}.<ref>Furthermore, these northern Yōrō River kofun clusters are not the tombs of a chieftain but of a small power; for this reason, besides the theory that the "king" of the king-gift inscribed iron sword was the {{ill|great king|ja|治天下大王|lt=great king|lt_ja=大王}} of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}}, there is also a theory that it was the person buried in the Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun on the opposite bank, a chieftain of Kami-tsu-Unakami.</ref> Close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}} from early on are therefore assumed. The first of the {{ill|Eastern Songs|ja|東歌 (和歌)|lt=Eastern Songs|lt_ja=東歌}} in Book 14 of the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'' reads, "Natsuso hiku Unakami-gata no oki-tsu-su ni fune wa todomemu sayo fuke ni keri" ("I shall moor the boat on the offshore shoal of Unakami-gata, where the summer hemp is pulled — night has already deepened"), and it is also considered to have been a key point of {{ill|water transport|ja|水上交通}}.

Furthermore, because the territory of the {{ill|Muza no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武社国造}} lies between this kuni no miyatsuko's territory and that of the {{ill|Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|下海上国造}}, the development of the Bōsō kuni no miyatsuko system is thought to have proceeded in at least two stages, and there is a theory that up to the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, together with Shimotsu-Unakami Province, it was a great power ruling an area from central Chiba Prefecture to {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}.

Afterwards, in the {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}, it was divided into upper and lower by Muza Province, founded by a power advancing from the central court, and was further forced to decline by the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|菊麻国造}} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|村田川}} basin. The {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Cluster|ja|姉崎古墳群}} on the lower south bank of the Yōrō River is considered the burial place of this kuni no miyatsuko's clan; following the 130-m-long {{ill|Anesaki-Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|姉崎天神山古墳}} of the latter half of the {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} and the 86-m-long Shakayama Kofun of the first half of the 5th century, the 103-m-long {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|ja|姉崎二子塚古墳}} was built in the latter half of the 5th century; the gradual shrinking of kofun said to date from the 6th century onward also attests to the decline.

=== Clan ===
The ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' names {{ill|Take-Hiratori-no-Mikoto|ja|建比良鳥命}}, son of {{ill|Amenohohi|ja|アメノホヒ|lt=Amenohohi-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=天穂日命}},<ref>Take-Hiratori-no-Mikoto is also called Take-Hinatori-no-Mikoto, Ame-Hinatori-no-Mikoto, Ame-no-Hiteri-no-Mikoto, etc.</ref> as the ancestor of the Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. According to the ''{{ill|Kokuzō Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}'', {{ill|Oshitachi-no-Katabi-no-Mikoto|ja|忍立化多比命}}, an 8th-generation descendant of Amenohohi-no-Mikoto, was appointed kuni no miyatsuko during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, and his grandson {{ill|Kuzuki-no-Atai|ja|久都伎直}} became Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}. The {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|出雲国造|lt=Izumo clan|lt_ja=出雲氏}}, of the same lineage as the {{ill|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}}, {{ill|Muzasa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造|lt=Muzashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt_ja=无邪志国造}}, {{ill|Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|下海上国造}}, {{ill|Ijimi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|伊甚国造|lt=Ijimu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt_ja=伊自牟国造}}, {{ill|Tōtsuōmi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|遠淡海国造}}, and others, is thought to have held the post of kuni no miyatsuko hereditarily. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'' records in the entry for the 22nd day of the 9th month of Jingo-Keiun 1: "To {{ill|Hinokuma-no-Toneri-no-Atai Takemaro|ja|檜前舎人建麻呂|lt=Hinokuma-no-Toneri-no-Atai Takemaro|lt_ja=檜前舎人直建麻呂}}, Outer Junior Fifth Rank Lower, a person of Unakami District in Kazusa Province, was granted the Kazusa-no-Sukune surname"; and in the entry for the 2nd day of the 3rd month of Hōki 6: "Kazusa-no-Sukune Takemaro, Outer Junior Fifth Rank Lower, was made Hayato-no-shō." These are thought to be descendants of the Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko and are also considered ancestors of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|上総氏}}.

Furthermore, at {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}}, Oshitachi-no-Katabi-no-Mikoto — appointed Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko — is said to have enshrined Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto and the Sai-no-Mihashira-no-Kami, and his grandson Oshikane-no-Mikoto to have jointly enshrined {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|ja|仁徳天皇|lt=Ōsazaki-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=大雀命}}; the Anesaki Kofun Cluster is said to be the burial place of the Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko's clan, who worshiped Anesaki Shrine as their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}}.

== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist}}

== References ==
*''Kokushi Daijiten'' ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}})
*Sakamoto Tarō, Hirano Kunio, ''Nihon Kodai Shizoku Jinmei Jiten'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan)
*''Nihon Kodai Jinmei Jiten'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan)
*''Nihon-shi Kō-jiten'' ({{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}})
*''Shintō Daijiten'' (Rinsen Shoten)
*''Nihon-shi Sōran'' ({{ill|Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha|ja|新人物往来社}})
*{{ill|Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男}}, ''Koshi to Izumo no Yoake'' (Hōrei Shuppan), 2008.

== See also ==
*{{ill|List of Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|国造の一覧}}

== External links ==
* [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/kamitu.html Walking the Kofun of Bōsō: Kofun of Kami-tsu-Unakami Province]

{{DEFAULTSORT:かみつうなかみのくにのみやつこ}}
[[Category:日本の歴史関連のスタブ項目]]
[[Category:上総国]]
[[Category:千葉県の歴史]]
[[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]]
[[ja:上海上国造]]
